findings.description: get() calls _purge_expired() on every lookup, turning the hot-path O(1) dict read into a full O(n) scan plus list allocation over all sessions, so n requests each scan n sessions.

_purge_expired() does a full O(n) scan and allocates a throwaway 'dead' list on every invocation; because it is invoked from read paths (get/active_count) this dominates aggregate cost instead of being amortized via lazy or timer-based eviction.
active_count() triggers a full O(n) purge scan with list allocation on every call, which is costly if polled by a metrics/dashboard endpoint.
user_sessions() is an unindexed O(n) linear scan over all sessions; with no user_id->tokens index it scales poorly if called on login/enforcement paths.

_new_token() rebuilds the alphabet constant per call and generates per-character via a Python-level comprehension; random.choices(alphabet, k=TOKEN_LENGTH) with a module-level alphabet is a single C-level call and faster.
Session tokens use the non-cryptographic random module instead of secrets, making tokens predictable (deferred to critic-security; noted because it co-locates with the token perf nit).
touch() does not purge and can slide expiry on an already-expired session that get() would report as gone, yielding inconsistent expiry semantics (deferred to critic-logic).
